questions.q: How does 'IJ' sound in Belgian Dutch?
questions.explanation: 'IJ' and 'EI' both sound like English 'eye'. Examples: zijn [zayn], tijd [tayd], klein [klayn]. IJ and EI are pronounced identically.

options: No — both have a long 'o' sound
questions.explanation: The sound is IDENTICAL. 'Boom' uses 'oo' in a closed syllable. 'Bomen' uses one 'o' in an open syllable (bo-men) — also long. Spelling changes, not sound.

questions.q: How do you pronounce 'kamer' in natural Belgian Dutch?
questions.explanation: Unstressed -er endings reduce to a schwa ('uh'). The R is nearly silent. 'kamer' = [KAH-muh], 'water' = [VAH-tuh], 'lekker' = [LEK-uh].

options: The neutral 'uh' sound that replaces unstressed 'e'
questions.explanation: The schwa [ə] is the neutral 'uh' sound. Every unstressed 'e' in Dutch becomes a schwa — 'de' = [duh], 'goedemorgen' = [khoo-duh-MOR-khun].

questions.q: How do you pronounce the Belgian 'g' in 'goed'?
options: A soft, breathy rasp at the back of the throat
Silent — the g is not pronounced
questions.explanation: The Belgian 'g' is a soft, gentle rasp at the back of the throat — like fogging a mirror. Much softer than Dutch Netherlands.

questions.q: 'bom' vs 'bommen' — what does doubling the 'm' signal?
options: The vowel is long in the plural
The vowel stays short — doubling prevents the open syllable from making it long
questions.explanation: Without doubling, 'bomen' reads as long o. 'Bommen' keeps the syllable closed (bom-men), preserving the short 'o'. Spelling protects the sound.
